clean_story:
Victors’ ink. Erased voices.

"History" comes from the Greek historia—meaning "inquiry" or "knowledge acquired by investigation." But empires treat it like a draft, editing out what doesn’t fit their story.

Conquerors don’t just take land; they take narratives. The British Museum holds the Rosetta Stone, carved in 196 BCE under Ptolemaic Egypt—yet its display frames it as a "discovery" by French scholars in 1799, not an artifact of African scholarship. Names matter: The "Indus Valley Civilization" was renamed by British archaeologists in the 1920s, though locals called it Meluhha—a word erased from textbooks. Statues help: Rome recycled Greek gods as their own (Zeus became Jupiter), while Spain’s conquistadors melted Inca gold into coins, turning sacred objects into currency.

Schools teach this edited version. In 1930s Japan, textbooks called Korea "a backward land civilized by Japan," though records show Korea’s Goryeo dynasty invented metal movable type in 1234—200 years before Gutenberg. The U.S. still debates whether to call 1492 a "discovery" or an invasion, though Taino people had named the Caribbean Borikén long before Columbus.

Three related subjects are stolen artifacts repatriation, language as colonial tool, and how museums shape memory.
